user_query:
I’m a chef supporting a heart transplant recipient, and I keep some simple local files to manage their meal plans. I’m worried I’ve made risky choices that could expose sensitive info or create safety issues. Please do a security and risk assessment of my setup using only the files in this workspace and produce structured outputs and a concise report.

Inputs to use:
- input/config/app_settings.yaml
- input/scripts/export_meals.py
- input/data/meal_plans.jsonl (each line is one JSON object)

What I need:
1) Inspect the configuration and script for risky patterns.
   - Review input/config/app_settings.yaml and input/scripts/export_meals.py for data protection weaknesses.
   - Identify: hard-coded secrets, disabled or lax security settings, risky sharing defaults, and insecure file permission usage or PII logging.
   - For each finding, include: "source" (exact file path), "setting_or_pattern" (e.g., yaml key or code constant/function), a short "value_or_snippet" excerpt, "risk" (1–2 line explanation), "severity" (High|Medium|Low), and a concise "recommendation".
   - Treat these specifically as risks if present: link sharing enabled, external domains allowed for sharing, default edit permissions, retention > 365 days, backups disabled, 2FA disabled, default/weak passwords, API keys or secrets in config/code, logging of PII, and any world-readable/writable permissions (e.g., 0o777).

2) Analyze meal plan records for exposure and nutritional risk metrics.
   - Parse input/data/meal_plans.jsonl and compute these metrics:
     • total_records
     • num_records_shared_externally (a record counts if shared_with contains any email whose domain is not "clinic.org")
     • num_records_link_shared (count where share_via_link == true)
     • unique_external_domains (set of domains from shared_with that are not "clinic.org")
     • num_records_with_phi_in_notes (notes contains the substring "MRN:" case-insensitive)
     • sodium_threshold_mg = 1500 (use this exact threshold)
     • num_records_over_sodium_threshold (sodium_mg > 1500)
     • mean_sodium_mg and median_sodium_mg (for even counts, median is the average of the two middle values)

3) Write the following outputs (do not modify inputs):
   - outputs/security/findings.json with keys:
     • config_issues: [ ... ] (array of objects with the fields defined in step 1)
     • code_issues: [ ... ] (array of objects with the fields defined in step 1)
     • data_exposure_summary: object with the metrics from step 2 related to sharing and PHI exposure
     • nutrition_risk_summary: object with sodium_threshold_mg, num_records_over_sodium_threshold, mean_sodium_mg, median_sodium_mg
   - outputs/security/aggregates.csv containing two columns: metric,value, with one row per numeric metric computed in step 2.
   - outputs/security/risk_report.md: a concise narrative (aim for 1–2 pages) that:
     • summarizes the top 3 highest-severity risks from the config/script with context
     • presents the exposure and nutrition risk numbers in plain language
     • provides at least 5 actionable, prioritized recommendations tied to the specific settings/patterns you found
     • includes a short Status Update section that references the generated files (outputs/security/findings.json and outputs/security/aggregates.csv) and proposes next steps

Assumptions & constraints:
- Treat only the domain "clinic.org" as internal; all other email domains are external.
- Use solely the provided files—no external lookups and no additional data.
- Keep outputs deterministic and reproducible from these inputs.
- Do not change any input files. Only create the outputs at the specified paths.

Thank you for helping me quantify and reduce both data exposure and nutrition-related risks. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
